stories.title: Standing Up for What's Right
stories.story:
During recess, Youssef noticed a group of older boys surrounding a younger student near the school fence, laughing at him and mocking the way he spoke. The younger boy, who Youssef recognized as a new student, looked like he was about to cry, but nobody around seemed to be doing anything about it.

Youssef's heart raced. He wasn't friends with any of the older boys, and part of him wanted to simply walk past and pretend he hadn't noticed — it would have been so much easier. But he kept thinking about how he would feel if nobody ever stood up for him.

Taking a deep breath, Youssef walked over. "Hey, leave him alone," he said, trying to sound braver than he felt. The older boys turned to look at him, surprised that anyone would interrupt them. For a tense moment, nobody spoke. Then, one of the older boys shrugged and said, "Whatever, it was just a joke," and the group walked away.

Youssef turned to the younger student, whose name was Fady, and asked if he wanted to sit together at lunch. Fady's relieved smile told Youssef everything he needed to know. Later, their teacher pulled Youssef aside and told him that what he had done took real courage — not the loud kind of courage people usually talk about, but the quiet kind that happens when nobody's watching and you do the right thing anyway. From that day on, Youssef and Fady became close friends, and Youssef never forgot how one small decision had made such a big difference.

stories.title: The Letter I Never Sent
stories.story:
Nadia and her best friend Rana had been inseparable since kindergarten, but everything changed the summer before eighth grade when Rana's family moved to another city for her father's new job. At first, they messaged each other every single day, sharing every small detail of their lives.

But as weeks turned into months, the messages became shorter, then less frequent, until eventually they stopped altogether. Nadia told herself Rana was just busy adjusting to her new school and new friends, but deep down, she felt a quiet ache of loss that she didn't know how to talk about with anyone.

One evening, Nadia sat down and wrote Rana a long letter — not a text message, but an actual handwritten letter, describing how much she missed their friendship and how strange it felt to navigate middle school without her. Writing it made Nadia feel better, even before she sent it. But when she finished, she hesitated. What if Rana had genuinely moved on and didn't feel the same way? What if sending it made things awkward?

Nadia folded the letter and placed it in her desk drawer, unsent, telling herself she would send it "eventually." Weeks later, to her complete surprise, Nadia received a package in the mail from Rana — inside was a letter almost identical to her own, describing the exact same fears and the exact same sadness about losing touch. That night, Nadia finally mailed her letter too. Some friendships, she realized, don't end because people stop caring — sometimes they just need someone brave enough to speak first.
